title: Key Ideas
text:
When to compare two groups
The most basic statistical question is: are these two groups different? When we have a continuous outcome measured in two populations (e.g., room and board costs at private vs. public colleges), the two-sample t-test gives us a formal framework to answer this.


The two-sample t-test
The test compares two sample means and asks whether the difference is larger than we’d expect from random variation alone. The key assumptions are:

Observations are independent within and between groups
Each group is approximately normally distributed (or samples are large enough for CLT)
For the classic test: equal variances; for Welch’s test: variances can differ

When variances differ, use Welch’s t-test (the default in R’s t.test()). You can check with an F-test or simply by comparing the group standard deviations.


One-sided vs. two-sided tests

Two-sided: \(H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2\) vs. \(H_a: \mu_1 \neq \mu_2\)
One-sided: \(H_0: \mu_1 \leq \mu_2\) vs. \(H_a: \mu_1 > \mu_2\)

Use one-sided only when you have a directional hypothesis before seeing the data.


Permutation tests (randomization tests)
When normality assumptions are questionable (e.g., skewed distributions), permutation tests provide a non-parametric alternative:

Compute the observed test statistic (e.g., t-statistic)
Shuffle the group labels many times (e.g., 10,000 permutations)
Recompute the statistic each time to build a null distribution
Compare the observed statistic to this null distribution

The p-value is the proportion of permuted statistics as extreme as (or more extreme than) the observed one. This approach makes no distributional assumptions — it directly tests whether the group labels matter.


Connection to the exercises
In Assignment 1, you’ll apply these ideas to the College dataset: comparing room/board costs and top-10% enrollment between private and public colleges. Question 1 uses the standard t-test (justified by approximate normality), while Question 2 introduces the permutation approach when the distribution is skewed.
